Re: 
Your Request For Advice, Our 
Advice No. A-84-007 
Dear Mr. Amerikaner: 
Thank- you for your request for advice on behalf of Santa 
Barbara City Councilman Sid Smitn. 
The facts, as stated in your 
letter, are that Mr. Smitn owns seven rental units within the 
City limits. 
He has owned tne units for approximately 15-20 
years and rents them on a month-to-month tenancy.ll 
His 
tenants have lived in tne units for between 5 and 15 years and 
Mr. Smith has never had to terminate a tenancy. 
During 1984, tne Santa Barbara City Council will consider 
adopting a "Just Cause for Eviction" Ordinance.£! 
This will 
require a landlord to specify in writing his or her reason for 
seeking an eviction. 
The ordinance will also restrict a 
landlord's discretion by permitting evictions only for specified 
reasons.lI 
Your question is whether the Political Reform Act 
11 
The cumulative value of the units is $400,000. 
In 
1982, the net rental income from tne units was $20,000. 
Mr. Smith is not holding a first or last month's rent, nor a 
cleaning deposit for any tenant. 
21 
There has already been some discussion of the 
Ordinance. 
11 
Some of the reasons are: 
1) The tenant fails to pay 
the rent, habitually pays tne rent late, or gives checks which 
are frequently returned because of insufficient funds; 2) the 
tenant is using or permitting a rental to be used for an illegal 
purpose; or 3) the tenant refuses the landlord reasonable access 
to ~~e rental unit as provided by state or local law. 


Steven A. AmeriKaner 
Februa ry 6, 1984 
page 2 
allows Mr. Smith to participate in the City Council's decision 
on tne Ordinance. 
Dl.scussion 
Government Code Section 87100il provides that no public 
official shall make, participate in the maKing, or in any way 
attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental 
decision in which ne knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest. 
An offl.cial has a "financial interest" in a 
decision witnin the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that tne aecision will have a material financiar 
effect on: 
11 
11 
11 
(0) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more 
t.tia n $1, 00 0 • 
Section 87103(b) • 
Mr. Sml.th has real property interests in the seven rental 
units. 
A decision will have a "material financial effect" on 
these interests if it will increase or decrease: 
(A) 
The income producing potential of the 
property oy tne lesser of: 
1. 
$1,000 per month~ or 
2. 
5% per month if the effect is $50 or more 
per month; or 
(B) 
The fair market value of the property by the 
lesser of: 
1. 
$10,000; or 
2. 
One half of one percent if the effect is 
$1,000 or more. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702 (b) (2) (A) and (B).) 
4/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code unless otnerwise indicated. 


Steven A. AmeriKaner 
Feorua ry 6, 1984 
Page 3 
On the basis of tne facts presented to us, the proposed 
ordinance will not affect ~he income producing potential of 
Mr. Smitn's real property interests. 
The ordinance specifically 
states that "nothing {in particular subsections of the 
ordinance] snall effect a landlord's ability to determine, 
maintain or change the rent charged for the rental unit." 
In 
addition, Mr. Smith currently has no plans to evict any of his 
tenants and, thus, it is not foreseeable that the ordinance will 
cause him to incur any additional eviction expenses that will 
materially affect the income producing potential of a particular 
unit (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b) (2) (A}). 
However, if, at 
the time the Council considers the ordinance, these facts have 
changed, Mr. Smith can contact our office for further advice. 
It also does not appear that the ordinance will have a 
foreseeable material effect on the fair market value of 
Mr. Smitn's real property interests. 
The ordinance does not 
restrict his aoility to sell the property or alter its use. 
However, if based upon tne facts available to Mr. Smith, he 
determines tnat tne fair marKet value of his real property 
interests will be affected in the manner described in 2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 18702 (D) (2) (B), he must disqualify himself. 
If I can be of any further help to you, please feel free to 
contact me at 916/322-5901. 
Very truly yours, 
.z~h~~ 
Counsel, Legal Division 
JSM: Km 
, 


OFFICE OF THE CITY A TIORNEY 
January 9, 1984 
Janis Shank McLean, Esq. 
Counsel, Legal Division 
Fair Political Practices 
Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Dear Ms. McLean: 
CITY HAll 
DE LA GUERRA PLAZA 
P.O. DRAWER P-P 
SANTA BARBARA, CA 93102 
~ ~~) 963-0611 
~ 
~ 
I am directing this inquiry to you inasmuch as you have 
advised other city attorneys with respect to the applicability 
of the Political Reform Act of 1974 to certain situations 
involving rent control and related issues. 
This inquiry is on behalf of Santa Barbara City Councilmember 
Sid Smith. 
Mr. Smith presently owns (with his wife) seven 
rental units within the City limits. 
They have a collective 
present market value of approximately $400,000. 
The net rental 
income for the units in 1982 was $20,000. 
The Smiths have 
owned the units for approximately 15-20 years. 
The units are all approximately 75 years old, and are not 
subject to any existing encumbrances. 
Each is occupied by a 
tenant without a written lease (on a month-to-month tenancy). 
r-1r. Smith has never been called upon to terminate the tenancy 
of any renter, each of whom have been in his apartment for 
between five and fifteen years. 
He is not holding either 
first or last month's deposit for any tenant, nor any cleaning 
deposit. 
The Santa Barbara City Council has had under consideration for 
some months a "Just Cause Eviction" Ordinance. 
Consideration 
of the ordinance was deferred from June, 1983 to June, 1984 
while certain information gathering was undertaken. 
It is 
expected that the Council will again consider this ordinance 
during 1984. 


Janis Shank McLean, Esq. 
January 9, 1984 
Page 2 
The proposed ordinance would require any landlord seeking to 
evict a tenant to specify in writing the reason for that 
eviction. 
The ordinance would further restrict a landlord's 
discretion and would only permit evictions for specified 
reasons. 
A copy of the latest draft of the Just Cause Eviction 
Ordinance (as presented to the Council in June, 1983) is 
attached for your information. 
On behalf of Councilmember Smith, I am requesting your advice 
concerning whether he may participate in the City Council 
decision on the proposed Just Cause Eviction Ordinance when 
the matter comes before the Council. 
Thank you for your consideration of this request. 
Please let 
me know if you have any questions or wish to discuss the 
matter further with us. 
Very truly yours, 
/ STEVEN A. AMERIKANER 
City Attorney 
SAA:ab 
Enclosure 
cc: 
Councilmember Sid Smith

sections.question: Your question is whether the Political Reform Act allows Mr. Smith to participate in the City Council's decision on the Ordinance.
sections.conclusion: On the basis of the facts presented to us, the proposed ordinance will not affect the income producing potential of Mr. Smith's real property interests. The ordinance specifically states that 'nothing [in particular subsections of the ordinance] shall effect a landlord's ability to determine, maintain or change the rent charged for the rental unit.' In addition, Mr. Smith currently has no plans to evict any of his tenants and, thus, it is not foreseeable that the ordinance will cause him to incur any additional eviction expenses that will materially affect the income producing potential of a particular unit. It also does not appear that the ordinance will have a foreseeable material effect on the fair market value of Mr. Smith's real property interests. The ordinance does not restrict his ability to sell the property or alter its use.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a Santa Barbara City Councilman who owns seven rental units worth $400,000 can participate in the City Council's vote on a proposed 'Just Cause for Eviction' ordinance that would restrict landlords' ability to evict tenants.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Mr. Smith may participate in the Council's decision on the ordinance because, based on the facts presented, the proposed ordinance will not have a material financial effect on his rental property interests—either in terms of income-producing potential or fair market value. The ordinance explicitly preserves landlords' ability to set rents, and Mr. Smith has no current plans to evict tenants, making additional eviction expenses unforeseeable.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request letter (dated January 9, 1984) and FPPC response letter (dated February 6, 1984). Analysis is based on the FPPC response portion. Question and Conclusion sections clearly identified. Both verbatim and synthetic versions provided as requested.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your question is whether the Political Reform Act allows Mr. Smith to participate in the City Council's decision on the Ordinance.

CONCLUSION: On the basis of the facts presented to us, the proposed ordinance will not affect the income producing potential of Mr. Smith's real property interests. The ordinance specifically states that 'nothing [in particular subsections of the ordinance] shall effect a landlord's ability to determine, maintain or change the rent charged for the rental unit.' In addition, Mr. Smith currently has no plans to evict any of his tenants and, thus, it is not foreseeable that the ordinance will cause him to incur any additional eviction expenses that will materially affect the income producing potential of a particular unit. It also does not appear that the ordinance will have a foreseeable material effect on the fair market value of Mr. Smith's real property interests. The ordinance does not restrict his ability to sell the property or alter its use.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that Santa Barbara City Councilman Sid Smith, who owns seven rental units worth $400,000, may participate in the City Council's vote on a proposed 'Just Cause for Eviction' ordinance because the ordinance will not have a material financial effect on his real property interests under Government Code Section 87100. The analysis concludes that the ordinance does not restrict rent-setting or property value, and given Mr. Smith's longstanding tenants and lack of eviction history, no material financial impact is foreseeable.
